title: Bakewell tart
subtitle: British almond frangipane tart with raspberry jam in a shortcrust shell
excerpt: Classic British tart from Derbyshire with a layer of raspberry jam and a filling of almond frangipane baked in a shortcrust pastry shell. The frangipane sets to a dense, moist, almond-flavoured sponge that rises around the jam during baking. Finished with flaked almonds pressed into the top before baking and left to cool completely before slicing. Keeps well for several days and is better the next day.
sourceNotes: The Bakewell tart originates from the town of Bakewell in Derbyshire, England. The original Bakewell pudding is described in Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845, Project Gutenberg), as a pastry-lined tart with a filling of butter, sugar, eggs, and almonds over jam. The modern form with ground almond frangipane and flaked almond topping is consistent with British baking literature from the late nineteenth century. The dish is sometimes called Bakewell pudding in its original form.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the baked tart for up to 1 month. Defrost at room temperature; the frangipane softens slightly on freezing and is best served the day it is defrosted.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pastry can be made a day ahead. The baked tart keeps at room temperature for 3 days; it improves overnight as the frangipane settles.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 170°C for blind-baking; reduce to 180°C conventional (160°C fan) for the filled tart. Blind-bake the pastry case for 15 minutes, remove weights, bake for 5 minutes, then fill and bake for 25 to 30 minutes until the frangipane is set and golden and a skewer comes out clean.
baking.decoratingTechnique: flaked almond topping pressed into the frangipane before baking

recipeTools.notes: for blind-baking; fill with baking beans or dried rice

glossaryTerms.definition: Frangipane is an almond cream made from equal weights of butter, sugar, eggs, and ground almonds, with a small amount of flour for stability. It is not the same as marzipan: frangipane is soft and baked, marzipan is dense and raw. During baking, frangipane puffs up around the jam and sets to a moist, dense sponge texture. A properly baked frangipane is set throughout with no wet centre; a skewer comes out clean and the surface is a deep even gold. Underbaked frangipane is gummy in the centre and slumps on cooling.

content.text: The key to a good Bakewell tart is a fully baked blind-baked case and a correctly set
. The jam layer should be thin and even; too much and the frangipane will not adhere to the base. Cool completely before slicing.

Make the pastry: rub the cold butter into the flour and icing sugar until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs. Add the egg yolk, salt, and 1 to 2 teaspoons of cold water and mix until the dough just comes together. Flatten into a disc, wrap, and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 190°C conventional (170°C fan). Roll the chilled pastry and line the tart tin. Prick the base all over. Line with baking paper, fill with baking beans or dried rice, and blind-bake for 15 minutes. Remove the paper and weights and bake for a further 5 minutes. Reduce the oven to 180°C conventional (160°C fan).
Make the frangipane: beat the softened butter and sugar until pale. Add the eggs one at a time, beating well between each addition. Fold in the almond flour, plain flour, and vanilla.

Spread the raspberry jam in a thin, even layer over the pastry base. Spoon the frangipane over the jam and spread it level. Scatter the flaked almonds over the top. Bake at 180°C for 25 to 30 minutes until the frangipane is set, golden, and a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean. Cool completely in the tin on a rack before slicing.
